coredata.dc:title: Target-oriented synthesis of high synthetic yield carbon dots by waxberry for optical dual-mode and smartphone imaging detection of morin
coredata.dc:creator: Tang, Siyuan
Li, Xiameng
Wang, Changxing
Li, Tingting
Guo, Guoqiang
Wang, Yiru
Chen, Da
coredata.dc:description: Recently, the use of natural products for the synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) has received much attention due to its peculiar characteristics. Herewith, the novelty of our present work is the green, large-scale production of fluorescent nitrogen doped CDs (N-CDs) synthesized from the waxberry by simple hydrothermal treatment. The current method delivers a high yield of 71% and the N-CDs exhibits 484 nm emission wavelength for detection of morin. Meanwhile, the increase of morin concentration can cause significant color changes. Hence, an optical dual-mode sensor for morin detection in the range of 0–35 μM with a limit of detection (LOD) of 56 nM and a smartphone imaging method are developed, which will broaden the application range of N-CDs in on-site detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Large-scale production
Waxberry
Morin detection
Ratiometric sensor
Smartphone imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The ratiometric fluorescent detection of morin
Quantification of morin by using a smartphone

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: K C Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of N-CDs (Inset: the corresponding particle size distribution). (b) The HR-TEM image of N-CDs. (c) FT-IR spectrum of N-CDs. (d) UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of N-CDs. (e) Emission spectra of N-CDs at different excitation wavelengths. (f) Excitation-emission matrix graph map for N-CDs.

(a) The emission wavelength of N-CDs with different concentrations of morin. (b) The relationship between F484/F560 and the concentration of morin. Inset: the corresponding linear plot. (c) The selectivity test of N-CDs. (d) Fluorescence color change of solution and film with different morin concentration. (e) Reproducibility of N-CDs response to morin. (f) Color scale card by using the images in panel (d). (g) Plot of RGB values and morin concentrations. Inset: the corresponding linear plot.
Schematic illustration of N-CDs for the synthesis route and the application in morin sensing.

head.ce:title: Target-oriented synthesis of high synthetic yield carbon dots by waxberry for optical dual-mode and smartphone imaging detection of morin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering, School of Physical Science and Technology, Ningbo University, Ningbo 315211, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering
School of Physical Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering, School of Physical Science and Technology, Ningbo University, Ningbo 315211, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, the use of natural products for the synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) has received much attention due to its peculiar characteristics. Herewith, the novelty of our present work is the green, large-scale production of fluorescent nitrogen doped CDs (N-CDs) synthesized from the waxberry by simple hydrothermal treatment. The current method delivers a high yield of 71% and the N-CDs exhibits 484 nm emission wavelength for detection of morin. Meanwhile, the increase of morin concentration can cause significant color changes. Hence, an optical dual-mode sensor for morin detection in the range of 0–35 μM with a limit of detection (LOD) of 56 nM and a smartphone imaging method are developed, which will broaden the application range of N-CDs in on-site detection.

ce:section.ce:para: Morin is one of naturally flavonoid compounds, which is found in vegetables, fruits and many Chinese herbs [1,2]. Morin is generally viewed as potential therapeutic drugs due to many special effects, such as anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, anti-diabetic and anticancer [1–3]. Meanwhile, morin is considered as a dietary factor that can improve vascular function in diabetes. Hence, the detection of morin is of great significance in monitoring the health of humans [3]. Until now, professional detection methods for trace morin include high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), electrochemical and nuclear magnetic spectroscopy [2,3]. Though these techniques can detect trace morin with high sensitively and selectively, sample preparations and operation of sophisticated instruments are expensive and time consuming, which limit their on-site and instant detection applications [2–4]. Therefore, it is extremely important to develop a rapid, low cost, high sensitivity and high selectivity detection method for morin.

Currently, the fluorescence technique has caught tremendous attention due to its great selectivity, fast response and superior sensitivity. Nanoparticles have opened up a new way for fluorometry due to their unique spectroscopic, electronic and chemical properties [4,5]. Among these nanoparticles, carbon dots (CDs), as a rising star of fluorescent nanoprobe, have superb benefits including excellent optical properties, low toxicity and chemical stability [6]. For example, Zhang et al. adopted 2, 4-dinitrobenzene- functionalized CDs for the detection of thiophenols [6]. Preethi et al. fabricated carbon dots for sensitive detection towards cobalamin [7]. However, these CDs detect substances by changing the fluorescence intensity of a single emission, which has defects in data and visual analysis in applications [5]. In addition, the widespread applications of CDs are greatly restricted due to their low yield and non-visual detection. Therefore, exploiting a novel CDs with high yield and visual detection capability is of highly important concern.

Herein, the bio-friendly N-CDs are obtained through single-step hydrothermal route from waxberry (Scheme 1 ), which possess a high yield of 71% and emission wavelength at 484 nm. With the addition of morin, a ratiometric detection with a linear range from 0 to 35 μM is achieved, and the detection limit is 56 nM. Meanwhile, the solution shows a visual color change from cyan to yellow. As a result, the sum RGB values of the fluorescent image can be recognized by a smartphone application for visual quantitative detection.

The waxberry is bought from the local supermarket. Morin and other chemicals are purchased from Aladdin. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum is performed on a Bruker Tensor II FT-IR spectrometer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images are recorded on JEM-2100. The fluorescence spectra are measured by Hitachi F-7000 instrument.

N-CDs are prepared via a hydrothermal method from waxberry. 5.0 g waxberry flesh is crushed and dissolved in 20.0 mL deionized water by stirring and then heated at 160 °C for 8 h. After the autoclave is naturally cooled to room temperature, the obtained solution is orderly treated by centrifuging (7000 rpm, 10 min), dialysing (MWCO: 100–500 Da, 48 h) and lyophilizing to obtain the solid powder of the N-CDs.
The detection of morin, HPLC test and smartphone operation are shown in Supporting information.

In this work, the yield of N-CDs from waxberry by hydrothermal is 71%. The amplified preparation can reach kilogram level, which lays an important foundation for massive applications of carbon dots in various fields. The morphology and size of N-CDs are explored through TEM. As illustrated in Fig. 1 (a), the N-CDs possess good mono-dispersity, and the HR-TEM image (Fig. 1(b)) shows that the lattice spacing is 0.24 nm, corresponding to the (100) lattice stripe of N-CDs [6,8]. The N-CDs have a narrow range distribution from 0 nm to 7 nm and possess an average diameter of 3.2 nm.

N-CDs are further evaluated by FT-IR analysis (Fig. 1(c)), and the broad peaks at 3100–3500 cm−1 and 2750–3000 cm−1 correspond to O-H/N-H stretching vibrations of hydrogen bond and C-H stretch [6]. Also, the occurrence of a prominent peak at 1647 cm−1 corresponds to the C=O stretch bond, while weak peaks at 1486 cm−1 and 1006 cm−1 are ascribed to C–N and C–O stretching vibrations, respectively [5].

In UV–vis absorption spectrum (black line), the two absorption peaks located at 230 nm and 280 nm are ascribed to π-π* of C=C bond and n-π* of C=O bond (Fig. 1(d)) [6]. Under the UV light, the N-CDs solution shows cyan emission. The fluorescence spectrum exhibits the optimum excitation and emission wavelength at 400 nm (orange line) and 484 nm (green line). As depicted in Fig. 1(e), the emission wavelength of N-CDs behaves an excitation-dependent feature when the excitation wavelength is changed from 350 nm to 450 nm. Fig. 1(f) reveals the two-dimensional mapping of N-CDs, which shows an emission peak at 484 nm under the excitation of 400 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The ratiometric fluorescent detection of morin
ce:section.ce:para: The quantitative capability of N-CDs for morin detection is evaluated. As displayed in Fig. 2 (a), with the increasing of the morin concentration, the fluorescence intensity of 484 nm gradually decreases and exhibits an increase at 560 nm. The sensing mechanism is ascribed to internal filtering effect (IFE) and electrons transfer. The detailed description is expressed in Supporting information (Figs. S1-S2). By calculating the emission intensity ratio of F484/F560 (F484 and F560 represent the fluorescence intensities at 484 nm and 560 nm) versus the concentration of morin from 0 to 200 μM, a linear relationship is obtained in the range of 0–35 μM with R2 = 0.984 (Fig. 2(b)). The LOD is estimated to be 56 nM. In addition, the selectivity of N-CDs towards morin is assessed by comparing with metal ions, amino acids and flavonoids-like compounds in Fig. 2(c). There are not obvious fluorescence changes under identical conditions, which indicate that N-CDs exhibit the high selectivity towards morin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of morin by using a smartphone
ce:section.ce:para: With the enhancement of fluorescence peak at 560 nm, the visual detection process under UV light can be observed in Fig. 2(d). Meanwhile, N-CDs also have excellent response in fluorescent film. Subsequently, we develop a handheld smartphone detection device based on the color change of N-CDs (Fig. S3). Reproducibility of N-CDs response to morin (10 μM) reveal a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 0.32% (n = 6 times), which confirms that this analytical method is reproducible (Fig. 2(e)). Furthermore, the RGB values of the images are transformed to corresponding color scale card and evaluated with app (Fig. 2(f)). The relationship between RGB values and the morin concentrations is depicted in Fig. 2(g). From the plot, a linear range of 0–30 μM is obtained and the LOD is calculated to be 61 nM. In addition, we also measure morin in real samples by using HPLC and fluorescence techniques to validate the practicability of N-CDs. The percentage recoveries are in range of 96.9–107.6% and %RSD is lower than 4.65, indicating that our proposed method has excellent accuracy and reliability (Table S1). The t-test shows no significant differences between both methods at a 95% confidence level.

In this paper, we introduce a biological friendly and large-scale production of N-CDs from waxberry by a simple hydrothermal method. The green synthesized N-CDs show cyan fluorescence along with a high yield of 71%. With the addition of morin, the fluorescence spectra of N-CDs change from a single peak emission to double peak emissions (484 nm and 560 nm) under single wavelength excitation, and the constructed ratiometric probe shows a linear range from 0 to 35 μM with a detection limit of 56 nM. Furthermore, a handheld smartphone sensing device is designed based on the visual fluorescence change. By analyzing the RGB values, a low detection limit of 61 nM is achieved and the method has been validated for monitoring morin in real samples. Hence, the large-scale production of N-CDs has broad application prospects for real-time detection in food safety monitoring and environmental pollution analysis.

Siyuan Tang: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Xiameng Li: Data curation, Investigation. Changxing Wang: Investigation, Methodology. Tingting Li: Investigation. Guoqiang Guo: Investigation. Yiru Wang: Investigation. Da Chen: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by projects from National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant (No. 61604084). K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2022.132673.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Long-wavelength excitation of carbon dots as the probe for real-time imaging of the living-cell cycle process

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Li, H.B. Wang, J.Q. Guo, S. Ye, W.L. Shi, X. Peng, J. Song, J.L. Qu, Long-wavelength excitation of carbon dots as the probe for real-time imaging of the living-cell cycle process, Sens. Actuator B-Chem. 311 (2020) 127891.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrophilic nitrogen-doped carbon dots from biowaste using dwarf banana peel for environmental and biological applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Atchudan, T.N.J.I. Edison, S. Perumal, N. Muthuchamy, Y.R. Lee, Hydrophilic nitrogen-doped carbon dots from biowaste using dwarf banana peel for environmental and biological applications, Fuel 275 (2020) 117821.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots as an effective fluorescence probe for morin detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L.Z. Liu, Z. Mi, Q. Hu, C.Q. Li, X.H. Li, F. Feng, Green synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots as an effective fluorescence probe for morin detection, Anal. Methods 11 (2019) 353-358.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots derived from Phyllanthus acidus utilized as a fluorescent probe for label-free selective detection of Fe3+ ions, live cell imaging and fluorescent ink

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Atchudan, T.N.J.I. Edisona, K.R. Aseer, S. Perumal, N. Karthik, Y.R. Lee, Highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots derived from Phyllanthus acidus utilized as a fluorescent probe for label-free selective detection of Fe3+ ions, live cell imaging and fluorescent ink, Biosens. Bioelectron. 99 (2018) 303-311.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Inhibition of oxidative stress in vivo through enzyme-like activity of carbon dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Dong, S.N. Wang, M.H. Ma, P.F. Wei, Y. Chen, A.G. Wu, Z.B. Zha, H. Bi, Inhibition of oxidative stress in vivo through enzyme-like activity of carbon dots, Appl. Mater. Today 25 (2021) 101178.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon-dot-based colorimetric turn-on probe for selectively detecting thiophenols

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.R. Zhang, Q. Wang, Z.F. Liu, J. Liu, J.F. Lu, Carbon-dot-based colorimetric turn-on probe for selectively detecting thiophenols, Mater. Lett. 277 (2020) 128390.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An environment-friendly route to explore the carbon quantum dots derived from curry berries (Murrayakoenigii L) as a fluorescent biosensor for detecting vitamin B12

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Preethi, C. Viswanathan, N. Ponpandian, An environment-friendly route to explore the carbon quantum dots derived from curry berries (Murrayakoenigii L) as a fluorescent biosensor for detecting vitamin B12, Mater. Lett. 303 (2021) 130521.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Utilization of waste biomass of Poa pratensis for green synthesis of n-doped carbon dots and its application in detection of Mn2+ and Fe3+

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Krishnaiah, R. Atchudan, S. Perumal, E.S. Salama, Y.R. Lee, B.H. Jeon, Utilization of waste biomass of Poa pratensis for green synthesis of n-doped carbon dots and its application in detection of Mn2+ and Fe3+, Chemosphere 286 (2022) 131764.
